title: New Findings on the Effect of Grape Powder on Human Skin After Acute Exposure to UVB. In previous studies, we have demonstrated that consumption of grapes is capable of enhancing the skin's ultraviolet resistance in approximately 1 in 3 subjects, 31 percent, based on increases in the minimal erythema dose. Naturally, the clinical protocol used to monitor the minimal erythema dose changes was designed to produce no serious damage to the skin, for example, first-, second-, or third-degree burns. As indicated by the name of the evaluation procedure, ultraviolet exposure was minimal. To approach maximal redness, a dose of about 6 to 10 minimal erythema doses is required. The broader consequences of greater sun exposure have been reviewed. In the context of a minimal erythema dose, however, although an increase resulting from a dietary intervention may imply greater skin resilience to sun exposure, the actual test parameter is simply the minimal visible response induced by a low dose of ultraviolet irradiation, which results in redness caused by engorgement of capillaries.

sentences.text: New Findings on the Effect of Grape Powder on Human Skin After Acute Exposure to UVB.

In previous studies, we have demonstrated that consumption of grapes is capable of enhancing the skin's ultraviolet resistance in approximately 1 in 3 subjects, 31 percent, based on increases in the minimal erythema dose.
Naturally, the clinical protocol used to monitor the minimal erythema dose changes was designed to produce no serious damage to the skin, for example, first-, second-, or third-degree burns.
As indicated by the name of the evaluation procedure, ultraviolet exposure was minimal.
To approach maximal redness, a dose of about 6 to 10 minimal erythema doses is required.
The broader consequences of greater sun exposure have been reviewed.

In the context of a minimal erythema dose, however, although an increase resulting from a dietary intervention may imply greater skin resilience to sun exposure, the actual test parameter is simply the minimal visible response induced by a low dose of ultraviolet irradiation, which results in redness caused by engorgement of capillaries.
The overall study design used to investigate the potential of grape consumption to affect the minimal erythema dose is outlined.
In brief, subjects were exposed to ultraviolet to determine minimal erythema dose values on day 15 and 16.
This determination was repeated on day 30 and 31, after the subjects had consumed grapes equivalent to three servings per day for the intervening two-week period.
Skin biopsies were examined from the site not exposed to ultraviolet irradiation on day 15 and from the ultraviolet-irradiated site and again on day 31, following grape consumption.

Based on the discussion provided above, a priori, it was not anticipated that the histological evaluation would reveal any significant findings, protective or otherwise, in terms of ultraviolet response or in terms of grape consumption.
This proved to be the case.
There were no obvious changes in control samples due to grape consumption, nor were there any obvious alterations in ultraviolet-treated specimens as a result of grape consumption.
It was beyond the scope of our clinical protocol to determine the potential of grapes to protect against higher doses of ultraviolet irradiation.

Thus, to investigate the potential of grape consumption to modulate the skin's response to ultraviolet irradiation in greater detail, both in subjects demonstrating enhancement of minimal erythema dose values and in those showing no response in terms of minimal erythema dose, we evaluated some molecular markers using immunohistochemical analysis.
A preliminary account is presented here.
In brief, using biopsies obtained from ultraviolet-exposed skin, both before and after a two-week period of consuming grapes, immunohistochemical images were acquired, and the images were quantified.

The biomarkers investigated included malondialdehyde, a byproduct of lipid peroxidation that serves as a marker for oxidative stress; Bcl-2 levels, an anti-apoptotic marker; gamma H 2 A X, an early indicator of D N A double-strand breaks; C O X-2, a marker for inflammation and oxidative stress; p53, which induces cell cycle arrest, D N A repair, and apoptosis; C O X-1, another anti-apoptotic marker; and Fontana-Masson staining for melanin, the production of which is triggered by ultraviolet irradiation.
The interrelationships of these markers are illustrated.
The overall results are summarized.
As noted above, 33 percent of the subjects in the study demonstrated an increase in the minimal erythema dose following grape consumption.

However, in terms of biomarkers, with both groups, those showing increased minimal erythema dose values following grape consumption and those not showing an increase, as a result of grape consumption, more than 30 percent of the subjects showed modulation of biomarkers that are indicative of amelioration of sun damage.
In fact, the percentage of subjects who consumed grapes but showed no change in the minimal erythema dose test generally exceeded the percentage of responders, in terms of biomarker modulation.

These data suggest that increased resistance to ultraviolet irradiation based on increases in minimal erythema dose values may not be fully representative of the magnitude of the impact of grape consumption.

On balance, the results of biomarker modulation suggest that at least twice as many individuals are responsive to grape consumption in terms of their response to ultraviolet irradiation, more likely, around 75 percent when considering the data as a whole.
Further examination of our data did not show any major correlation of biomarker change based on sex or Fitzpatrick skin type.
Nor does any obvious pattern of biomarker modulation emerge when comparing minimal erythema dose nonresponder males with minimal erythema dose nonresponder females.
Differences in response appear to be due to inter-individual variations.
We know that alteration of phenotypic expression varies from person to person and continues to vary in different manner on an individual basis following grape consumption.
As shown below with a single individual, gene expression in the skin varies, based solely on grape consumption as well as on ultraviolet exposure.

Taken together with complex signaling induced by ultraviolet radiation, the dynamic influence of the gut microbiome, metabolomic alterations, potential pharmacogenomic effects, and a myriad of other factors, it is likely that some type of pharmacogenomic paradigm would need to be devised to predict responsiveness.
